questions.stem: Which term is best described as follows: the practice of understanding a culture by its own standards rather than judging it by another culture's?

choices.label: A
choices.text: cultural relativism

choices.label: B
choices.text: stigma

choices.label: C
choices.text: assimilation

choices.label: D
choices.text: globalization

questions.explanation: This description matches cultural relativism, which is the practice of understanding a culture by its own standards rather than judging it by another culture's. The other options name different concepts that do not fit this description.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes cultural diffusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The spread of cultural traits, ideas, or objects from one group or society to another.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An organized, sustained effort by many people to promote or resist social change.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A group within a society that shares distinctive norms and values while sharing the larger culture.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deviance that follows after a person adopts a deviant identity in response to labeling.

questions.explanation: Cultural diffusion is best described as the spread of cultural traits, ideas, or objects from one group or society to another. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of folkways?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The near-universal horror people feel toward cannibalism.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wearing very casual clothing to a formal dinner and drawing disapproving looks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A teenager acting out more after being repeatedly branded a troublemaker by adults.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A movement that rejects mainstream consumerism and lives communally off the grid.

questions.explanation: An example of folkways is: wearing very casual clothing to a formal dinner and drawing disapproving looks. In general, folkways refers to informal everyday norms whose violation brings mild disapproval rather than serious punishment. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which term is best described as follows: strongly held norms with moral significance whose violation brings serious condemnation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: globalization

choices.label: B
choices.text: mores

choices.label: C
choices.text: culture

choices.label: D
choices.text: ethnocentrism

questions.explanation: This description matches mores, which is strongly held norms with moral significance whose violation brings serious condemnation. The other options name different concepts that do not fit this description.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes deviance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The tendency to evaluate other cultures by the standards of one's own culture, seen as superior.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The specific convictions that members of a culture accept as true.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The rewards and punishments a group uses to enforce conformity to its norms.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Any behavior, trait, or belief that violates a society's norms and draws a negative response.

questions.explanation: Deviance is best described as any behavior, trait, or belief that violates a society's norms and draws a negative response. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of anomie?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The near-universal horror people feel toward cannibalism.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The smartphones, cars, and clothing produced and used within a society.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A community losing its shared rules and sense of order after sudden upheaval.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A group that treats refusing paid work as admirable rather than irresponsible.

questions.explanation: An example of anomie is: a community losing its shared rules and sense of order after sudden upheaval. In general, anomie refers to a condition of normlessness in which social norms are weak, unclear, or conflicting. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of the culture of poverty?

choices.label: A

choices.label: C

In general, the culture of poverty refers to the contested idea that poverty persists through values passed down among the poor. The other options illustrate different concepts.

questions.stem: A study reports that the difference between two groups has a p-value of 0.03, using the common 0.05 threshold. Which interpretation is most accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The result is not statistically significant and should be ignored.

choices.label: B
choices.text: There is a 97 percent chance that the hypothesis is true.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The result is statistically significant, meaning a difference this large is unlikely if only chance were operating.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The result proves the researcher's hypothesis with certainty.

questions.explanation: Because 0.03 is below the 0.05 threshold, the result is statistically significant: a difference this extreme is unlikely under chance alone. It does not prove the hypothesis or give the probability that it is true.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of the bystander effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No one in a large crowd helping because each assumes someone else will.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A young child assuming everyone sees exactly what they see.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Parents who enforce strict rules with little explanation or affection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A toddler exploring freely, then calming quickly when the parent returns.

questions.explanation: An example of the bystander effect is: no one in a large crowd helping because each assumes someone else will. In general, the bystander effect refers to people being less likely to help a victim when other people are present. The other options illustrate different concepts.
